prompt: Complete: 'Ho cominciato ____ l'italiano due anni fa.' (studiare)
explanation: 'I started studying Italian two years ago.' Some verbs carry a fixed little word before a following infinitive; the fixed word is that verb's governed preposition, and for cominciare it is a: cominciare a studiare. The preposition is not guessable from meaning, so each verb's choice has to be learned; di (the other big family) is the classic wrong reach here.

'This summer I learned to swim.' Imparare belongs to the a-family of governed prepositions: imparare a nuotare. Learning-type and starting-type verbs (imparare, cominciare, provare, riuscire) cluster in this family, which helps the memory even though each verb still has to be confirmed individually.

'I can't manage to understand this word.' Riuscire takes its governed preposition a before an infinitive: riuscire a capire. Non riesco a... is the everyday way of saying can't manage to; the a is fixed and di in its place is the standard miss.

any_phrases.note: understood, but before another a- sound the euphonic ad is standard

explanation: 'Try to open the window.' Provare takes a before an infinitive, and when the next word also starts with a-, the a grows a d for flow: ad aprire. This euphonic d is the same one seen in ed before e-. Plain 'a aprire' is understood but sounds clumsy; di aprire picks the wrong family altogether.
examiner_note: Graded acceptance: a aprire at 0.9 per the standard-variant policy (secondary but current).

explanation: 'Will you help me carry the suitcases?' Aiutare governs a before the infinitive: aiutare qualcuno a fare qualcosa. English 'help someone do' has no preposition at all, so the trap here is dropping the word rather than picking di; both miss the governed a.

translation.reveal: continuare - to continue, to keep on

explanation: 'The child keeps on crying.' Continuare governs a: continuare a piangere. Keep-on / carry-on meanings sit in the a-family; di piangere is the standard wrong reach.

'I finished working at eight.' Finire governs di before an infinitive: finire di lavorare. Finire is the mirror of cominciare a, and the pair is worth learning together precisely because the prepositions differ; a lavorare here borrows the wrong family.

translation.reveal: cercare - to try (cercare di); to look for

explanation: 'We are trying to solve the problem.' Cercare on its own means to look for, but cercare di + infinitive means to try to: cercare di risolvere. The di is what flips the meaning, so getting it wrong is not cosmetic; a risolvere mixes in the provare/riuscire family.
'We decided to stay in Italy.' Decidere governs di: decidere di restare. Deciding, finishing, trying-in-the-cercare-sense and stopping all live in the di-family; a restare is the cross-family miss.

'My father stopped smoking.' Smettere governs di: smettere di fumare. Note the asymmetry with the a-family: continuare a (keep on) but smettere di (stop); the two opposites sit in different preposition families, which is exactly why per-verb learning is unavoidable.

'Don't forget to close the door!' Dimenticare governs di before an infinitive: dimenticare di chiudere. English 'forget to' tempts a (to = a is the beginner's mapping), but the governed preposition ignores that logic; it is di, learned per verb.

'I often think of you.' Pensare takes a before the person or thing on your mind: pensare a qualcuno. English 'think of / about' pulls hard toward di, but pensare di is a different construction (having an opinion, or planning to do something); for the have-in-mind sense the governed preposition is a.

any_phrases.note: articulated form; specific rather than generic reference, equally correct

explanation: 'We talked politics for hours.' Parlare marks its topic with di: parlare di politica. Generic di politica and specific della politica are both correct here; what the verb fixes is the di itself, and a politica misfiles the topic as a destination.

'I need help right away.' Avere bisogno marks what is needed with di: ho bisogno di aiuto. Because English 'I need help' has no preposition at all, the commonest miss is simply dropping the di; per aiuto (from 'need for help') is the other reach. The di is part of the construction, not optional.
examiner_note: attempted_hints ['aiuto'] catches the dropped-di answer ('Ho bisogno aiuto') as attempted; the positive phrase is checked first so the correct answer is unaffected.

prompt: Complete: 'Marco si è innamorato ____ ragazza inglese.' (una)
explanation: 'Marco fell in love with an English girl.' English says in love WITH, so con is the magnetic wrong answer; Italian's governed preposition is di: innamorarsi di qualcuno. This is the clearest case of the per-verb rule beating translation instinct.

any_phrases.note: uncontracted: credits the di-choice; the missing contraction is markpoint B's miss

explanation: 'What do you think of the new teacher?' Asking for an opinion flips pensare into its di construction: pensare di qualcuno/qualcosa. Then di meets the article il and contracts: di + il = del, giving del nuovo professore. Compare prep_vg_obj_01: thinking OF someone (having them in mind) is pensare a; thinking something OF them (opinion) is pensare di.
examiner_note: Two markpoints, per one-markpoint-per-skill: A credits the di-choice even uncontracted (di il nuovo hits A, misses B), mirroring prep_pl_06; al nuovo misses A but hits B (wrongly chosen, correctly contracted). Uncontracted a il misses both.

explanation: 'I live in Florence with my family.' Towns and cities take a: a Firenze, a Roma, a Londra. English uses in for both cities and countries, so 'in Firenze' is the magnetic miss; Italian reserves in for the bigger or more enclosed places.
examiner_note: Earlier frame ended 'da tre anni', priming a da answer whose superstring da Firenze would false-credit; the family frame removes the primer.

explanation: 'My sister lives in Germany.' Countries take in: in Germania, in Italia, in Francia. The split with towns (a Firenze but in Germania) is the core place rule; a Germania applies the town preposition one size up.

'We spend August in Tuscany.' Regions pattern with countries, not towns: in Toscana, in Sicilia, in Umbria. So the dividing line is not Italy-vs-abroad but size-class: town-sized places take a, region-sized and up take in.

'The kids are at the library.' Familiar enclosed spaces take bare in, no article: in biblioteca, in ufficio, in chiesa, in banca. English 'at the library' invites an article or an a; the idiomatic pattern is the bare in.

prompt: Translate the ending: 'Tonight we're having dinner at Marco's.' → 'Stasera ceniamo ____ .'

any_phrases.note: the spelt-out form; equally correct, da Marco is the compact idiom

explanation: 'Tonight we're having dinner at Marco's.' A person's place is marked with da: da Marco means at (or to) Marco's home. English has the possessive ('at Marco's') where Italian has this special da; a Marco would mean dinner is somehow directed at Marco himself. The spelt-out a casa di Marco says the same thing in full.

any_phrases.note: credits the place rule only; the missing contraction is markpoint B's miss

explanation: 'My cousin works in the United States.' Countries take in, and a plural-named country carries its article, so in meets gli and contracts: in + gli = negli, giving negli Stati Uniti. Two things to get right at once: the country preposition (not a) and the contraction (not in gli).
examiner_note: Two markpoints: A credits choosing in for a country even if left uncontracted; B credits the contraction. 'in gli Stati' hits A, misses B; 'agli Stati' misses A but its contraction is not creditable to B since the preposition is wrong at source, so B's any_phrases deliberately excludes it (unlike prep_vg_obj_05 where either contraction credits B; here the wrong-prep form is also the natural English calque and would over-credit).

translation.reveal: tornare - to return, to go back

explanation: 'I'm going back to Italy in December.' Destination follows the same size-class rule as location: countries take in even when you are moving towards them, so tornare in Italia. The a already visible in 'a dicembre' belongs to the month, not the country.
'We're in Venice for the weekend.' Venice is a city, so a Venezia. City-sized means a, however famous or watery the city; in Venezia is the same miss as in Roma.

'The lesson finishes at nine.' Clock times take a plus the article, contracted: a + le = alle, so alle nove. The article is not optional; bare 'a nove' and uncontracted 'a le nove' are the two halves of the same miss.
examiner_note: Frame uses finire, not cominciare: 'comincia dalle nove' is producible colloquial Italian and dalle nove contains alle nove, which the positive-first engine would false-credit (superstring hazard, cf. the PresentUsageAuthor thread).

explanation: 'I always have lunch at one sharp.' One o'clock is the odd one out: the hour is singular, so the article is l' (not le), and a + l' = all', giving all'una. Alle una drags the plural pattern onto the one hour that is singular.
examiner_note: norm() folds apostrophes to spaces (Architecture, batch review v1), so all'una and all' una normalise identically; single any_phrase suffices. 'In punto' guards the frame against a from-one reading whose dall'una would contain all'una.

any_phrases.note: article instead of di; equally idiomatic for habitual time. Word-anchored so alla/nella mattina cannot contain it.
regional (northern) but widespread; standard forms are di mattina / la mattina

explanation: 'I study better in the morning.' Parts of the day take di: di mattina, di sera, di notte. (Plain la mattina works too for a habit.) English 'in the morning' pulls toward in, which Italian does not use here.
'My birthday is in June.' Months take a or in, both fully standard: a giugno and in giugno. Di belongs to parts of the day, not months; di giugno on its own is the misapplied pattern.
examiner_note: Frame avoids verbs of closing/opening: 'chiudono da giugno' (since June) is a plausible learner product and da giugno contains a giugno (superstring hazard).

explanation: 'I've been living here since 2020.' A starting point that still holds takes da, contracted with the year's article: da + il = dal, so dal 2020. Note the tense logic: Italian keeps the present (vivo) where English says 'have been living'; nel 2020 would instead pin the event inside that year.
examiner_note: Single markpoint despite the contraction: dal + year is formulaic and the da-choice is the diagnostic; 'da il 2020' is caught as uncontracted.

prompt: Complete: 'Aspetto l'autobus ____ venti minuti e non arriva.'
explanation: 'I've been waiting for the bus for twenty minutes and it's not coming.' A duration still running at the moment of speaking takes da with the present tense: aspetto da venti minuti. English 'for' covers both this and finished durations, so per is the magnetic miss; per belongs to spans that are over (see the pairing with 'ho studiato per tre ore').

'Yesterday I studied for three hours.' A finished, measured span takes per: per tre ore. The mirror-image of the da-durative: da + present for still-running time, per + past for a span that is closed. One English 'for', two Italian prepositions, split by whether the clock is still ticking.

'The train leaves in ten minutes.' Time-until-something takes fra (or tra, identical in meaning and register): fra dieci minuti. English 'in ten minutes' makes in the natural wrong reach, but Italian's in dieci minuti answers a different question: how long something takes, not when it starts.

'I was born in 1998.' Years take in plus the article, contracted: in + il = nel, so nel 1998. The in instinct from English is right as far as it goes; the miss is stopping there, because the year's article must come along and fuse.

'Tomorrow evening we're going to the theatre.' Going to a place-as-activity takes bare a: a teatro, a scuola, a casa. No article, and not in; the destination is named for what you do there, and that family runs on a.

'This winter I'm going to Switzerland for work.' Movement toward a country takes the same in as being there: vado in Svizzera. Destination and location share one preposition in Italian, chosen by the size-class of the place, not by whether you are moving.

prompt: Translate the ending: 'The flight from Madrid arrives at six.' → 'Il volo ____ arriva alle sei.'
explanation: 'The flight arrives from Madrid at six.' Coming FROM somewhere takes da: arrivare da, venire da. The near-miss is di, which marks belonging rather than motion: sono di Madrid says Madrid is my hometown, vengo da Madrid says that is where I have just travelled from.
examiner_note: English anchor pins the origin reading; unanchored, 'arriva a Madrid alle sei' was fully correct Italian falling through unmatched (review finding 1). must_not 'a Madrid' is word-anchored so the correct 'da Madrid' cannot contain it.

explanation: 'Today at three I'm going to the dentist's.' Going to a person (or their practice, shop, home) takes da, here contracted with the article: da + il = dal dentista. English 'to' pulls toward a, but a person as destination is da territory, the same da as da Marco.

prompt: Translate the ending: 'Tomorrow morning we leave for Paris.' → 'Domani mattina partiamo ____ .'
explanation: 'Tomorrow morning we leave for Paris.' Partire marks its destination with per: partire per Parigi, exactly like English 'leave for'. The general go-to preposition a (vado a Parigi) does not follow partire; this is a verb-fixed exception inside the motion system.
examiner_note: Unanchored, 'partiamo da Parigi' was correct Italian that substring-matched must_not 'a Parigi' (false diagnostic, review finding 2). The anchor makes da wrong; word-anchoring stops the containment.

any_phrases.note: heard colloquially; in treno is the standard means expression

explanation: 'I go to work by train.' Means of transport takes bare in: in treno, in macchina, in autobus. English 'by train' offers no preposition to copy, and the reach for con (with) lands on the recorded classic 'con treno'; with the article (con il treno) it becomes merely colloquial rather than wrong.

'The children walk to school.' On foot is a piedi, the one means expression that takes a instead of in. The trap runs both ways: in piedi exists but means standing up, so 'vanno a scuola in piedi' says the children commute upright.

'I open the door with the key.' An instrument in the hand takes con: con la chiave, con il coltello. The lookalike a chiave belongs to the fixed idiom chiudere a chiave (to lock); outside that idiom the tool preposition is con.

'Speak slowly and calmly.' Manner from a noun is built with con: con calma, con attenzione, con piacere. Where English makes an adverb (calmly), Italian often prefers con + noun, and the preposition in that mould is fixed.

'Maria goes to university by bike.' The bicycle counts as a vehicle, so in bicicletta, despite the temptation to treat riding as foot-powered and reach for a. Among everyday means only piedi takes a (plus fixed idioms like a cavallo); everything with wheels or wings takes in.

'This is my father's car.' Possession is di: la macchina di mio padre, il libro di Anna. Italian has no apostrophe-s, so the possessor always follows with di; da mio padre would send the car to his house instead.

any_phrases.note: commercial/technical register (catalogue style); di legno is the neutral choice

explanation: 'I bought a wooden table.' Material is di: di legno, di lana, d'oro. Shop catalogues write in legno, which is fine in that register, but the neutral everyday form is di; da legno misreads material as purpose.

'Luca is taller than me.' Comparison before a noun or pronoun takes di: più alto di me. The rival che does exist in comparisons, but only between two of the same kind (two adjectives, two infinitives: più furbo che intelligente); before a plain pronoun it is di, and che me is the classic miss.

any_phrases.note: su + article, equally correct for aboutness

explanation: 'I'm reading a book on Roman history.' A book's subject is marked with di: un libro di storia. Su works too when articulated (un libro sulla storia romana); what fails is bare su without its article, the halfway house between the two correct forms.

'I'd like a glass of wine, please.' What a container holds is di: un bicchiere di vino, una tazza di tè. Its mirror twin is da: un bicchiere da vino is a wine glass, the empty object made for the purpose. Ordering un bicchiere da vino asks the waiter for glassware.

'The centre of Milan is beautiful.' Of-relationships with places are di too: il centro di Milano, le strade di Roma. Same possession pattern as with people; da Milano would mean the centre came from Milan.

'I have nothing to eat.' Something-to-do-something runs on da: niente da mangiare, molto da fare, qualcosa da bere. The infinitive after da names what the thing is FOR; di mangiare borrows the verb-governed pattern (finire di...) where it does not belong.

prompt: Complete: 'Per il matrimonio compriamo dodici bicchieri ____ .' (vino)
explanation: 'For the wedding we're buying twelve wine glasses.' An object defined by its purpose takes da: bicchiere da vino, camera da letto, occhiali da sole. The pair with di is exact and treacherous: da vino = made for wine (empty), di vino = full of wine. Buying twelve bicchieri di vino buys twelve servings.

'The Sistine Chapel was painted by Michelangelo.' The doer in a passive sentence is introduced by da: dipinto da Giotto, scritto da Dante. English 'by' has no cognate pull here, so the misses scatter to di (possession logic) and per; the passive agent is always da.

prompt: Complete: '____ , passavo le estati al mare.' (bambino)
explanation: 'As a child, I used to spend my summers at the seaside.' A stage of life is marked with da: da bambino, da giovane, da studente. English 'as a child' makes come the magnetic calque, but come compares; da here means 'in the period of being'. Note the natural pairing with the imperfect (passavo) for the habitual past.

prompt: Translate the ending: 'We stayed at Luisa's until midnight.' → 'Siamo rimasti ____ fino a mezzanotte.'
explanation: 'We stayed at Luisa's until midnight.' The same personal da works for being at someone's place, not just going there: siamo da Luisa, resto dal nonno. Location and destination share the da, just as a and in double for both; a Luisa would aim the staying at her rather than her home.

'This painting is loved by everyone.' Passive agent again, in an everyday present-tense shape: amato da tutti. Worth a second angle because di tutti is a real phrase (belonging to everyone) that means something else; after a passive participle, the by-phrase is da.

prompt: Translate the ending: 'I watched a documentary about Leonardo.' → 'Ho visto un documentario ____ .'
explanation: 'I watched a documentary about Leonardo.' Su covers on in both senses, physical (sul tavolo) and topical (a documentary ON someone): un documentario su Leonardo. Di would flip it to authorship, a documentary BY Leonardo.

'Are you coming to the cinema with us?' Con is the plain with of company: con noi, con Maria. Its base sense maps cleanly from English, which is why the simple form is easy and the traps live elsewhere (means of transport, where in takes over).
'This present is for you.' Per is the for of benefit and destination-of-purpose: per te, per la festa. A te would mark a giving-to (dare a), but with essere the beneficiary reading wants per.

prompt: Complete: 'La farmacia è ____ e la banca.' (il bar)
explanation: 'The pharmacy is between the bar and the bank.' Between is tra or fra, and the two are the same word in different clothes: identical meaning, identical register, choose whichever sounds better next to the following word (fra tre giorni avoids tra-tre). Believing they differ in meaning is itself the classic confusion.
'Wait for me in the car, I'll be right back.' In is the inside preposition: in macchina, in casa, in borsa. Here it is bare because macchina is one of the familiar-space nouns that skip the article.

'I give the book to Maria.' A marks the receiver: dare a, dire a, scrivere a. Per Maria would say the book is intended for her but changes the construction; the recipient of dare is always introduced by a.

prompt: Combine into the contracted form: 'di + il pane' → ____
explanation: del pane = of/some of the bread. Di + il fuses to del: the preposition and article become one word. Leaving them separate (di il) is the giveaway miss; dello borrows the wrong article (lo belongs before s+consonant, z, and friends).

prompt: Combine into the contracted form: 'a + lo stadio' → ____
explanation: allo stadio = to/at the stadium. A + lo fuses to allo, keeping the special article that stadio demands (s+consonant). Al stadio applies the plain-il contraction to a noun that never took il in the first place.

prompt: Combine into the contracted form: 'in + la cucina' → ____
explanation: nella cucina = in the kitchen. In changes shape when it contracts: in + la = nella (the n doubles and an e appears). It is the least guessable of the five contracting prepositions, so uncontracted in la is the common flinch.

prompt: Combine into the contracted form: 'su + l'autobus' → ____
explanation: sull'autobus = on the bus. Su + l' = sull', keeping the elided article that a vowel-initial noun takes: sull'autobus, sull'isola. Sul autobus swaps in the pre-consonant article where the vowel demands l'.

prompt: Combine into the contracted form: 'da + i nonni' → ____
explanation: dai nonni = at/to the grandparents' place. Da + i = dai. The article must match the noun: nonni takes plain i, so dagli (the gli form) over-corrects.

prompt: Combine into the contracted form: 'a + gli studenti' → ____
explanation: agli studenti = to the students. A + gli = agli; studenti begins with s+consonant so its plural article is gli, and the contraction carries that. Ai studenti keeps the contraction but loses the article agreement.

prompt: Combine into the contracted form: 'di + le ragazze' → ____
explanation: delle ragazze = of the girls. Di + le = delle. Feminine plural keeps le inside the fused form; dei ragazze pairs a masculine article with a feminine noun.

prompt: Combine into the contracted form: 'in + i parchi' → ____
explanation: nei parchi = in the parks. In + i = nei, the same shape-shift as nella: the in becomes ne- before the article. Negli belongs to gli nouns (negli uffici); parchi takes plain i.

prompt: Combine into the contracted form: 'su + la sedia' → ____
explanation: sulla sedia = on the chair. Su + la = sulla with a doubled l; the single-l sula is a spelling miss the doubling rule exists to catch. All the su contractions double: sul, sullo, sulla, sui, sugli, sulle.

'The cat sleeps on the sofa.' Su + il = sul, here doing its job inside a sentence rather than as a drill fragment: dorme sul divano. Same rule as the fragment items; the skill is producing it without the arrow.

'I'm leaving with my friends tomorrow.' The trick: con does not contract in modern Italian, so con gli amici stays two words (only col and coi survive as optional relics). Nor do per, tra and fra. Knowing which five prepositions fuse (di, a, da, in, su) includes knowing which three do not.
examiner_note: The dated forms cogli/colle are deliberately not in any_phrases; per the standard-variant policy they are obsolete rather than secondary. They fall through unmatched rather than being punished.

any_phrases.note: fully correct Italian; 0.9 because the partitive rendering is the drilled pattern here (0.9-for-dodges policy question raised with Architecture, batch-review thread v3)

explanation: 'I'd like some bread, please.' Some is the articulated di: del pane, literally 'of the bread'. Un po' di pane also works, but leaning on un po' everywhere is the recorded habit this bucket watches for; the partitive del is the native default.

any_phrases.note: fully correct Italian; 0.9 because the partitive rendering is the drilled pattern here (0.9-for-dodges policy question raised with Architecture, batch-review thread v3)
fully correct Italian; 0.9 because the partitive rendering is the drilled pattern here (0.9-for-dodges policy question raised with Architecture, batch-review thread v3)

explanation: 'I bought some strawberries for dinner.' The partitive agrees like the article inside it: fragole is feminine plural, so delle fragole. Dei fragole gets the some-idea right but the agreement wrong; bare di fragole drops the article the partitive is made of.

any_phrases.note: fully correct Italian; 0.9 because the partitive rendering is the drilled pattern here (0.9-for-dodges policy question raised with Architecture, batch-review thread v3)
fully correct Italian; 0.9 because the partitive rendering is the drilled pattern here (0.9-for-dodges policy question raised with Architecture, batch-review thread v3)

explanation: 'There are some biscuits on the table.' Masculine plural: dei biscotti. The partitive is just di + the noun's own article, so the whole definite-article system (il/lo/la/i/gli/le) rides along inside it.

any_phrases.note: fully correct Italian; 0.9 because the partitive rendering is the drilled pattern here (0.9-for-dodges policy question raised with Architecture, batch-review thread v3)

explanation: 'We saw some birds in the garden.' Uccelli starts with a vowel, so its article is gli and the partitive is degli uccelli. Dei uccelli is the agreement miss: the some-form must track the noun's article exactly, including the vowel rule.

prompt: a or in? 'Quest'anno passiamo le vacanze ____ .' (Sardegna)
explanation: 'This year we're spending the holidays in Sardinia.' Sardinia is region-sized, and region-sized places take in: in Sardegna, in Toscana. The a option belongs one size down, to towns.

'My brother studies in Bologna.' Bologna is a city, so a Bologna. English 'in' covers both sizes, which is exactly why the choice needs drilling: the Italian line runs between town (a) and region or country (in).

prompt: a or in? '____ d'estate fa molto caldo.' (Capri)
explanation: 'On Capri it's very hot in summer.' Small islands side with towns: a Capri, a Ischia. Large islands side with regions: in Sicilia, in Sardegna. The size-class rule runs right through the islands, and Capri sits on the town side of the line.

'This cheese comes from Naples.' With venire the meaning is source-of-movement, and source is da: viene da Napoli. Di marks belonging or identity, which is the neighbouring sentence 'è di Napoli' (it is Neapolitan); the verb decides which relationship is in play.

prompt: di or da? 'Sono ____ , ma vivo a Torino.' (Palermo)
explanation: 'I'm from Palermo, but I live in Turin.' Essere di names where you belong: sono di Palermo is an identity statement. Da would make it travel (vengo da Palermo, I've come from there). One English 'from', two Italian relationships, split by belonging versus movement.

prompt: di or da? 'Per colazione uso sempre la mia tazza ____ .' (tè)
explanation: 'For breakfast I always use my teacup.' Uso la mia tazza points at the object itself, the cup made for tea: tazza da tè. Di tè would fill it (a cup of tea), but you don't reuse a cupful every morning; the possessive and uso pin the purpose reading, and purpose is da.
